speaker: interviewer
text: Being in Brunnlitz with Schindler. And let me take you back a little bit. What happened before you came to Brunnlitz? Didn't you have to go to Auschwitz first?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Auschwitz? Yeah. There was no question if you have-- you have to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me-- well, tell me what happened. How did you come to go to Auschwitz, and what happened when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. They just-- they, they shaved your hair. They shaved-- they took everything away from you. They-- I didn't know where we are. Nobody knew. They put us, because we was the Schindler list, they put us in a, in a special big room over there. They took everything away. But we could, we could stay there. If it wouldn't be for Schindler, we would have gone straight to the oven. We would have gone. We wouldn't have-- we wouldn't have known where from what, nothing. In the morning, when the morning came, I said to my mother-in-law, look, we are here in front of the ovens. We didn't know. I, we, I got scared. Everybody got scared. We stood there in front, right in front of it, because we were not supposed to go to-- so that's what it was. Otherwise, I wouldn't be here. I wouldn't be alive. Nobody from the-- and this was all, this was all young people over there with me. All, all-- nobody knew nothing. But I-- we stood there, and we looked how it was burning. It was burning.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not there long. They took us to a real shower. And it was so cold. All the windows were open. Everything was open. And we didn't have nothing to wear, because they took everything away from us. So when we came out of the shower, we were all very, very cold. But then they gave us some kind of a-- something they gave us, something they gave us to put on. And we went to the trains. The trains came. And they took us to Brunnlitz. I, I didn't even know. We didn't know that we go to Brunnlitz. We didn't know that, that, that, that, that the whole thing was played up about-- is a man knows a man what did all this, all that thing. He knew what it was all about. His family was there, his--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which man are you talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: The man what took all the money and the-- he knew. He had all his family there. He had all-- and I remember, she slept in the same side where I-- she was-- she-- his mother-- his sister and his wife slept together. I remember. I remember them very well. And, and-- But I didn't know the other women very good. If you go to New York, you can find it from the, from the other part. But in New York lives there a couple. But was with me and with Richard, too, but he was from the beginning on. We are in touch all the time. We talk to each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: So going back to Brunnlitz and coming to the end, what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? End? End?

speaker: interviewer
text: The end, yes, near-- before the liberation. What happened exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing happened because-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember his name was Leopold. They wrote for him a-- it was all not, not, not really. They wrote a, a-- what do you call it here when you want to go to, to, to-- when you, when you, when you get an acknowledgment for that you have to go to the army? What you--

speaker: interviewer
text: A notice, a draft notice.

speaker: interviewee
text: A draft notice. They make a draft notice for him, for that Leopold. He wanted to kill us all. He wanted to put us somewhere in the-- somewhere and, and, and, and throw poison there. Anyways, they wanted-- he wanted to kill us. But the man who was there, the men from the other part of the Schindler list, said no. And they made, they made a false draft for him. And so he had to leave. He couldn't do it and he had to leave. But we didn't know that until, until, until late. But we didn't know that. And nothing happened. We didn't know. Right after when he left, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the Russians came in. The Russians came in, not the Americans. The Russians came in. And they liberated. They didn't know either when they came. And when they liberated us, they didn't know who we are, what we are, what it is, what-- they wanted to kill-- right away they wanted to kill a cow so we would have something to eat. [LAUGHS] I don't remember. They didn't kill that cow. I don't know. I didn't, I didn't know what they did. But they gave us to eat, yeah. They gave us something to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to Schindler at that point? Where was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schindler was-- all I remember, they put on, put on his-- things what man wore-- you know them.

speaker: interviewer
text: The uniform from the concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the things describes stuff from the-- but he was a, he was a husky, big man. So it didn't fit him. But OK, it didn't fit him. It didn't fit him. But they took him with the car. They took him to the, to the Austrian-- to the Czechoslovakia-Austrian border, because he could not to be in Czechoslovakia. He, he was a traitor for Czechoslovakia. Because when, when, when Hitler wanted to take over the Sudeten-- you know what the Sudeten is? Sudeten. He, he helped him. He helped him. So that's why he could go to Hitler and he could get anything he wanted. But they took him right away over there. When, when they talked, only that the war's over, when they talked over [INAUDIBLE]. Few, few of the guys, I don't know who, took him with a car, with a car and brought him to the Austrian border. And there they let him free.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he say anything to you people before he left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very much. Not very much. Nobody was supposed to know that. I saw him. I happened just to saw him outside in that outfit. I think, my god, what is he doing with that outfit? But they took him right away to the-- he could not stay in Czechoslovakia, because they would have, they would have cut him. And he wouldn't, he wouldn't have gotten out alive. He wouldn't be alive. So they took him over the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then the Russians came and liberated you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, the Russians were there. They liberated us. They did everything they could for us. And I can have to tell you, they did everything they could for us. They didn't know who we are. They didn't know what it is. They didn't know what, what, what we were doing here. So but they were, they were-- but then came the order that the camp will be liquidated, because there was nothing there no more. The camps will be liquidated. And, and they will have a train to go to Poland today, tomorrow, I forgot [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and I said, and I said, no, I don't go back to Poland. I go on the other side. I said, I, I, I-- what for? Where shall I go? I knew my parents were not alive. I didn't know that my brother is not alive. But I knew that my parents were not alive. Because then we all knew what, what they did with them. And I knew my parents went with one of the first transports but went to, to-- what is the name of that camp over there? [INAUDIBLE] was the first transport to-- next to Lublin, something. I will remind myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I said but my brother-- I was hoping, hoping, hoping that my brother is alive. And then I got a letter that my bother is not alive, that his wife is alive and his son is alive, but not him. He, he was an officer in the Polish, in the Polish army. So one, one tells me he was killed like this, the other one tells me he was killed like that. And the other one tells me-- everybody tells me something else. So I could never find out.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's on the list of the Polish, of the Polish men who served in the, in the war against the Nazis, in, in, in-- was it already in Poland where they were fighting, or in Russia and in Poland where they were fighting? And he was on that list. That's what, that's what they tell me. And I want to tell you something. My boyfriend was on the list, too. And where was your husband at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband, in that time, when he was liberated, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we were together already. We were already together.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war, how did you find your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you, you heard one, one piece, you heard. Well, he told me-- he told me, go and get your-- no. You know, Schindler had a big warehouse in the back of his factory over there. And, and the, the Germans stole everything they could. But they couldn't carry that. How far can they carry big, big things?

speaker: interviewer
text: So they brought it to Schindler. And he's a German. So they brought it to Schindler. And after the war was over, they would take it. But they didn't, they didn't win the war, and the war was not quite over. So he took it, and he divided it up between all of us. There were material to, to, to, to, to make a suit for men, for women, and several material for underwear-- you know, underwear. And there were material for--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was soap. Oh, was that soap was the biggest. Soap and, and, and, and, and thread to sew something up, and needles. Oh, needle-- oh, needles. It was the biggest thing. And, and, and what-- I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: One part my mother-in-law got, and the other part I got. And with that, we arrived in Praha. In Praha, I told you what happened. In Praha, we went in to, to eat dinner. And a man came in. And I said to him, my god. I said to my mother, my god, he was with us in the [INAUDIBLE] factory. What is he doing here? I went to him, and I said, remember me? I said. Yeah, what are you doing here? Go to your husband, he said. I said, where is my husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's in Linz in a hospital alive. Hardly alive, he said. Hardly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't go anymore to Poland. I went back to the camp. And when they, when they took everybody to Poland, I went to the, to the station. But I didn't go where they went. So again, a Russian came to me. And he said, what are you waiting here for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I don't want to go this way. I want to go the other way. I want to go to, I want to go to Austria. He said, it's not easy. But stay and wait here. I will help you. And he did. He helped me to get into the train. Well, when we came to Austria, was another chapter again. We were in Austria all the time until we got our affidavits to come to America. I had, I had permits to go to Australia, but in that time, it was no connection to go to Australia. It was no planes, no ships, no nothing, So we came to United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we came to the United States, we were-- we didn't know what to do. And first of all, they took away our papers. Well, you got scared. Why did you take away our papers? Why did [INAUDIBLE]? They didn't tell us right away. But later on we found out, when you came to a brother or to a sister, or to a mother or father, you didn't need no papers. My mother-in-law had here three brothers in that time. So we could go straight.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came. We came to, to-- we went with was the name of the ship. And that was it. We came. We came. It was very hard in the beginning, very, very, very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: I learned English fast. My husband didn't learn it so fast. I could, I could make it quicker than him because I, I learned the language quicker and I-- I had, I had one or two bad jobs. But then I landed a job in Swank. Have you ever heard from Swank? This is men's jewelry and wallets and things, and cuff links and this and that. So I came there to interview. He wanted to interview. So I came to interview. And he said, you don't look stupid. You will learn it very fast. That's how he told what he told me. You don't look stupid. You will learn very fast. Very good. And he turned right away and gave me the job.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked there for 10 years-- for 10 years. My boy was-- my bigger boy was born in that time. They gave me the whole layette, what you want and what you didn't want. They gave me everything I needed. And right after when I was feeling all right and feeling good, I went back to work. I didn't have to call, he wanted me back or not, no. Right away I said, Herman, I'm ready to work. Come back in the morning, he told me. And that's all. I learned very fast over there because everything was in, in codes over there. Everything had a code. A, a tie clip was a-- I forgot already. A tie clip was a G or something, and 250 was a B. And this is how the whole stock was in, in the code. Everything was in code. And you have to, you had to know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and they put me then-- I have to check-- order what goes out. What do I know to check order? What would I know? I didn't know anything. So there was another Jewish woman there, elderly woman. In that time, she looked to me elderly woman. And, and she started to explain to me what, what it is all about and what it is. And she start, and this is is this, and this is this. And this is how I learned over there. And I was the [INAUDIBLE] of my husband, made himself. He wanted to stay on his own feet, and he wanted to be a-- So he moved, we moved to Santa Monica. And from Santa Monica in that time, to go to downtown, that was, that was a big no-no. It was too far to go. So I had to quit. They waited for me a whole year to come back. A whole year they waited for me to come back. But I didn't go back no more, because I already had the small child. And it's close. How could I go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband started a, own business in that time already. So I wanted to help him out, even though there was not very much to help out, but I-- but he left the daily business, and-- because the daily business was-- now it became-- now it is more than it was then. It was, it was better. It was good, but you had-- we didn't have any money to go into business. And we didn't have any money to, to, to, to go in with somebody into business. So we had to think of something else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went to Page. In the meantime, Page came here. You know, we brought here three couples. You mean-- when you say Page, you mean Leopold Page?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He lived in New York with a man named Arthur Rent. And he wrote to us. We met him in, in, in, in, in New York. We didn't know him. But Page gave us his address, that this is his friend, we should go and visit him. So we visited him. And he said, where are you going? We said, we're going to, to, to, to California. He said, well, let us know how it is in California. I, I didn't know that man. I didn't know that woman. I didn't know-- they were strangers to us. When we arrived, we were here a little bit. We got a letter that they wanted to come here. They wanted to come here. They don't want to stay in New York. So we, we rented them a, a room. And, and they came. They came. After they came, they brought Leopold Page over here. And we are together ever since that. Ever since that time, we are friends. Ever since that time, we meet. We, we go out every, every week somewhere. And we-- when he goes away, he lets us know. And he doesn't do anything, I mean, not to let us know that he is. He's not. We are friends all the time. We stayed friends all the time. It's very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Mrs. Krumholtz, now that it's almost 50 years after World War II, is there a message that you'd like to give?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I would like to give a message that people should not be stupid and, and, and be-- there are churches, and they don't help. They don't help. A church doesn't help. They teach them still the same thing, what they taught 100 years ago-- hate. That's not right. That is not right. In Poland it was like that. In Poland they, they were teaching hate. That's not right. That's not right at all. Poles were very, very, very bad anti-Semites, very, very bad-- very bad. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Very bad. It's not right. Yeah, they should teach them to be human beings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Look what goes on now in the world, because they hate each other here and there and there and there. Look what goes on here and now. Look what goes on in England, how many people were killed in England because this is about religion? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] About religion, they were killed, because I don't even know what, are they Protestants or Catholics or something? They, they kill each other because of it. Is that-- is this, is this a way to do? Is this a way to do? It's not right. It's not right. And here in America, they have the Ku Klux Klan, and they have the, the other one. What is the other one? Ku Klux Klan, and the other one they have. Now they have-- I don't know what they call themselves, but same thing-- same thing-- same thing. They are against the blacks, and they are against this, and-- If you leave the blacks alone, they will, they will be normal people like-- you know how many blacks working in our stores? You know how many blacks are employed by us? Lots of blacks are employed by us. I don't know, I don't know exactly how many. But we don't have any trouble with them-- nothing. We don't have any, any, anything. They are not-- one is with us already since we opened the stores-- one. The other one, when they come, they stay. They don't go away. They don't go away.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: If you, if you are good to them, if you are, if you, I mean, are a human being to them, they are human being back. That's what I, we found out. If you, if you understand, if you handle them right like you should handle anybody else, they will not do anything bad to you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They are, they are, mostly are-- how shall I tell you that? Few of them get out very far. Few of-- I mean, they became doctors and lawyers and all this stuff and-- quite few of them became that.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other ones, I don't want to say that they are no good because they didn't became, the didn't became a doctor and a lawyer, but they are people. They are very decent people, some. It's a, it's a small minority who gets in trouble, that's all-- a small minority who gets in trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's not right. It's not right. You don't have to-- you have to be afraid to go in the evening on the street. That's not right. The street is made for people not to be afraid to walk on that. But they are-- they want money, they want money, and they cannot get a job. And when they get a job, they don't work right on the job, and things like that. And this is-- that's no good. And who knows if it ever will stop? Who knows? Who knows? And this is all over the, all over the world. Look what goes on in, in Romania. Was it, is it Romania? No, Yugoslavia. Look what goes on over there. Well, I will tell you something. You turn the cart around. You know what's a Yugoslav-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what do you call them? The thing what was together with them? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What is, who's fighting over there? In Bosnia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bosnia, who's fighting over there?

speaker: interviewer
text: The Serbs and the Croats.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Serbs? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So many Serbs went to the, to the camps, and they were killed. They were killed just because they're Serbs, because the Croats worked with Hitler. The Croats were with Hitler. And the Serbs get even now with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how many were killed during that war? An awful lot-- an awful lot. This is why-- this is a whole war over there, and it will not stop, because the Croats want to get even with them. But people don't know it here. But this is what it was, and this is what it is. This is not good.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did Stalin do? Stalin killed an awful lot of people-- an awful lot of people-- an awful lot of people, until, until until, until he got poisoned or something, I don't know. I think he got poisoned. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the second row, the first woman is my mother. Then is my father. And then the-- in the lower row, the second one is me. And on the third row, my, my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the far right?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the far right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where and when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, way, way, way before. I was a child here, before the war. I don't know what year exactly. I don't know the year. But it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The second one, the second from left, is my father. The other ones, this is the Biala rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: The one with the long beard is the Biala rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the one--

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to him, on the right here, is my, is my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many, many years before the war-- many, many years. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, who's this now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] Kornfeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken and where?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken at home, at home. But who knows when, what, what year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was home? What was the name of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bielsko-Biala. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband and me in Bindermichel, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: What is the year in Bindermichel?

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Oskar Schindler-- Oskar Schindler. He's the one what rescued us. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not so sure. But it must've been taken somewhere in, in, in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not so sure where it was taken, because it was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Mrs. Schindler, the wife of Oskar Schindler. This is Edzja Liebgold. We were together in camp from the first day to the last day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's me. And this was, was in, in New York-- in New York. What date? 1993.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Israel. In Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. This was taken in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Edzja Liebgold. This, I don't know, because she doesn't look at me like-- this is my husband, and this is Steven Spielberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the picture was taken in 1993?

speaker: interviewee
text: When was it? We were in, in Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Jerusalem, 1993.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is my husband and me on our wedding anniver-- which one? Which one?

speaker: interviewer
text: 52 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: 52 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Krumholtz, please introduce your husband to us. Introduce your husband to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband, Richard Krumholtz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why don't you just tell us what it's like being married almost 53 years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not easy. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you have to say, Mr. Krumholtz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a rough time for a few--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, very good. Very hard time.

speaker: interviewee
text: --beginning. But we are still happy marriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: The beginning was very hard here for us, very, very hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- up to around 1959, in this country, we had a very hard time. We couldn't make a living.

speaker: interviewee
text: But tell [INAUDIBLE], when did, when did we open up [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Then when it all came in. It came in.

speaker: interviewee
text: But up till '59, we had a rough time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then it came in all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now you enjoy your two sons--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, they're grown.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and your grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're grown up, our grandchildren and our two sons. And, and we are happy. We always were happy with each other. And our marriage was always good. It was always because of the children sometimes, because this, because of that. Well, you know, regular living things, but otherwise, we didn't have any problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
